title: Hospitals remove air conditioning in heatwave for health and safety reasons - The Telegraph
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
A hospital trust removed its mobile air-conditioning units during a recent heatwave because of health and safety concerns.
The units were deemed to present fire and infection control risks and were taken out of hospitals in Hampshire, leaving staff and patients sweltering in the June heatwave.
Hampshire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, which runs Basingstoke, Andover and Winchester hospitals, was unable to say how many air-conditioning units had been withdrawn.
An email sent by Tahir Akbar, the interim chief operating officer, said: “We can only use them in restricted circumstances, mainly to keep essential medication cool. If you need additional fans, please order through your divisional lead.”
He thanked staff for “working in these conditions”, adding: “I know that many of our wards and clinical areas are extremely uncomfortable for patients and staff.”
The trust provided staff with bottled water and ice lollies, and also allowed some home working.
Britain has seen successive heatwaves this year, with 28 days, consecutive and non-consecutive, where temperatures exceeded 30C somewhere in the UK.
There were 18 consecutive days of 30C-plus temperatures in the punishingly hot year of 1976, when drought conditions damaged crops, scorched landscapes and forced people to use standpipes in the street.
‘Care in challenging conditions’
A spokesman for the hospital trust said there were still some units available.
They said: “During periods of hot weather, we work really hard to minimise the impact of high temperatures across our hospitals and are always grateful to our brilliant colleagues who provide outstanding care for patients in challenging conditions.
“Across our sites, measures are put in place to support staff and patients, including distributing hundreds of fans, increasing deliveries of bottled water and providing refreshments such as ice lollies and ice cream to help colleagues stay comfortable during their shifts.
“Air-conditioning units are not without risk in a hospital environment, and in line with our commitment to maintaining the highest standards of safety for both patients and staff, their use is carefully assessed based on the needs of patients and the specific circumstances of each clinical area.
“Available units are deployed on a priority basis, particularly in critical locations where temperature control is essential, such as areas where medicines must be stored within strict temperature ranges.”
